description: The classic backyard staple. Tomatoes come in an enormous range of sizes, colours, and flavours. Most home varieties are indeterminate (keep growing and fruiting until frost), while some compact bush types are determinate (fruit all at once then stop). Full sun and consistent moisture are non-negotiable.
plantingGuide: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost. Transplant after all frost risk passes, burying the stem deep — up to the lowest leaves — as tomatoes root along the buried stem for extra stability and moisture uptake. Set stakes or cages at planting time before the roots establish.

description: The most productive tomato for home gardens. Vigorous indeterminate vines produce hundreds of bite-sized fruits over a long season. Very forgiving and great for beginners.

name: Roma Tomato
description: An Italian paste tomato with meaty, low-moisture flesh — ideal for sauces, pastes, and drying. Determinate plants fruit heavily over 3–4 weeks then stop, making it perfect for batch processing. Compact and sturdy, rarely needs staking.
notes: Allow fruits to ripen fully on the vine for maximum sweetness. Harvest all at once and process immediately for best results.

name: Beefsteak Tomato
description: The king of slicing tomatoes. Huge, meaty fruits with a classic rich tomato flavour. Indeterminate vines grow tall and need strong staking. Slower to mature than other varieties but the payoff is impressive.
notes: Water very consistently — uneven moisture causes blossom end rot and cracking in large fruit. Remove laterals (suckers) regularly for largest fruit.

name: Heirloom Tomato
fruitColour: multi — red, yellow, purple, green striped
description: Open-pollinated varieties passed down for generations. Incredible flavour diversity — from smoky Cherokee Purple to sweet Yellow Brandywine. Fruits are often irregular or cracked-looking but bursting with taste. Save seeds from year to year.
notes: More susceptible to disease than modern hybrids. Avoid overhead watering. Don't refrigerate harvested fruit — destroys flavour.

name: Yellow Pear Tomato
description: Adorable pear-shaped yellow cherry tomatoes with a mild, sweet flavour. Kids love them. Extremely prolific — expect hundreds of fruit per plant. The yellow colour means they're lower in acidity than red varieties.

Cucumbers are fast-growing warm-season climbers that reward with crisp, refreshing fruit. There are many types — from the familiar long telegraph to compact Lebanese and round apple cucumbers. All love heat, consistent water, and something to climb.
plantingGuide: Direct sow 2–3 seeds per position at 2cm depth once soil is consistently above 18°C. Thin to the strongest seedling. Erect a trellis or netting before planting and guide the first tendrils onto it — cucumbers need something to grab from the start.

name: Lebanese Cucumber
description: Short, thin-skinned cucumbers that don't need peeling. Crisp texture and mild flavour. Extremely popular in Australia. Pick at 10–15cm for best results — they turn bitter if left too long.

name: Telegraph Cucumber
description: The long, straight supermarket-style cucumber. Smooth dark green skin and mild flavour. Vigorous climber that needs a sturdy trellis. Pick when dark green — yellowing means overripe and bitter.

name: Apple Cucumber
description: Round, apple-shaped cucumbers with a very mild, almost sweet flavour. Thin skin and fewer seeds than standard cucumbers. Less bitter and more digestible — great for people who find regular cucumbers hard on the stomach.

Lettuce is one of the easiest and most rewarding vegetables to grow. It thrives in cool weather and is perfect for 'cut and come again' harvesting. Different types have very different textures, heat tolerance, and flavour — from crunchy cos to soft butterhead to loose-leaf oak.
plantingGuide: Sow very shallowly at 3mm depth in loose, moisture-retentive soil — lettuce needs light to germinate. Transplants and direct sowings both work. Sow successionally every 2–3 weeks for a continuous harvest. In summer, choose a shaded spot or use 30% shade cloth to delay bolting.

name: Cos / Romaine Lettuce
description: Tall, upright heads with crunchy, ribbed leaves and a slightly bitter flavour. The classic Caesar salad lettuce. Slower to bolt than loose-leaf types. More heat tolerant than butterhead. Harvest the whole head or peel outer leaves as needed.
notes: Blanch the inner leaves by tying outer leaves together for 5 days before harvest for milder flavour.

name: Butterhead Lettuce
description: Soft, buttery leaves with a mild, sweet flavour — the gentlest of all lettuces. Forms a loose, round head. The large cup-shaped leaves are perfect for wraps and san choy bau. Bolts quickly in heat — best as an autumn/winter crop.
notes: Keep well watered in warm spells to delay bolting. Harvest in the morning when leaves are crisp.

name: Oak Leaf Lettuce
description: Deeply lobed, oak-shaped leaves in green or bronze-red. One of the best cut-and-come-again lettuces — harvest outer leaves continually without pulling the whole plant. Slow to bolt, very productive, and looks beautiful in the garden.
notes: Plant in succession every 3 weeks for a constant supply. Red varieties develop more intense colour in cooler temperatures.

name: Iceberg Lettuce
description: Dense, crispy heads with a satisfying crunch. Takes longer than other lettuces but produces large, firm heads. More difficult to grow well than loose-leaf types — needs consistent moisture and good air circulation to prevent tip burn. The supermarket staple.
notes: Wrap outer leaves loosely around the developing head to encourage it to heart up. Harvest before warm weather causes bolting.

description: Chillies come in an extraordinary range of heat, shape, and colour — from mild banana peppers to face-melting Carolina Reapers. All love warm weather and full sun. They're perennial in frost-free climates and often produce for years.
plantingGuide: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost — chillies need a long warm season. Harden off seedlings over 1–2 weeks before transplanting. Plant into warm, fertile soil only; cold soil at transplanting stunts growth permanently. Hot varieties need an extra 2–4 weeks of warmth.

name: Cayenne Chilli
description: The workhorse chilli of the kitchen. Long, thin red peppers with a sharp, penetrating heat. Perfect for drying and grinding into cayenne powder. Very productive plants. Commonly used in Asian and Mexican cooking. Medium heat makes it versatile without being overwhelming.
notes: Dry whole pods hanging upside down or in a dehydrator. Can be frozen whole for long-term storage.

name: Jalapeño
description: The world's most popular chilli. Thick-walled, juicy pods with a moderate heat that mellows with cooking. Pick green for a grassy flavour or leave to ripen red for sweeter, hotter fruit. Excellent for pickling into jalapeños en escabeche or smoking into chipotles.
notes: Smoked and dried ripe jalapeños = chipotles. Allow a few pods to ripen fully red for seed saving.

name: Habanero
flavour: intensely fruity with fierce heat — 100,000–350,000 Scoville
description: Deceptively fruity and aromatic before the heat hits. Small, lantern-shaped pods pack an intense punch. Habaneros need a long, hot summer and take longer than most chillies to mature. Extremely productive once established — one plant will likely be more than enough.
notes: Use gloves when handling. Leaves and stems also contain capsaicin. Very slow to germinate — start seeds indoors 10–12 weeks before transplant.

name: Bird's Eye Chilli
bestUse: Thai cooking, stir-fries, chilli sauce, drying
description: Tiny but ferociously hot. The classic Thai and Southeast Asian chilli. Plants are perennial in warm climates and can produce thousands of small pods per season. Very drought-tolerant and low maintenance once established.
notes: In warm climates, cut back hard in late autumn and the plant will reshoot and produce again next season. Excellent in pots.

description: Capsicums (bell peppers) are the sweet, mild cousins of chilli. They need a long warm season to develop fully coloured fruit. Green capsicums are unripe — red, yellow, and orange are the same fruit left to ripen, and are sweeter and more nutritious.
plantingGuide: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost. Transplant into warm, well-drained, fertile soil after all risk of cold has passed. Place stakes at planting time. A later planting in warm soil always outperforms an early planting in cold soil — patience pays off.

name: Red Capsicum
flavour: sweet, rich, slightly smoky when roasted
description: The sweetest and most nutritious stage of the bell pepper. A fully ripened green capsicum left on the plant another 3–4 weeks. Higher in vitamin C than an orange. Rich, sweet flavour — excellent roasted, grilled, or stuffed. Needs a long hot season.
notes: Pick green for quicker harvest or leave to ripen to red/yellow/orange for sweeter, richer fruit. Once picked, doesn't continue ripening off the vine.

description: Pumpkins are vigorous, sprawling vines that take up a lot of space but reward with impressive harvests of long-keeping fruit. Different varieties range from small and sweet to enormous. Most store for months — some over a year — making them a valuable food security crop.
plantingGuide: Direct sow 2–3 seeds in a prepared 'hill' — a mound of soil amended with plenty of compost — after last frost. Thin to the strongest 1–2 plants. Site the hill at the bed edge so vines can run out onto grass or a path. Allow at least 2m between hills.

name: Butternut Pumpkin
description: Australia's most popular pumpkin. Smooth, beige skin and brilliant orange flesh. Sweet, dense texture that's perfect for soup and roasting. Stores well for 3–6 months in a cool, dry place. The compact teardrop shape is easier to cut than round varieties.
notes: Leave the last 5cm of vine attached to the fruit when harvesting (the 'handle') — this helps it store longer. Cure in the sun for 10 days before storage.

name: Kent / Jap Pumpkin
description: The round grey-green pumpkin found in every Australian supermarket. Vigorous vine that needs lots of space. Sweet, dry-textured orange flesh that roasts beautifully. Very long storage life — up to 12 months. A staple crop for serious vegetable gardeners.
notes: Thump the skin — it should sound hollow when ripe. The grey surface will develop a slight blush of orange. One of the most disease-resistant varieties.

description: Basil is the king of summer herbs. It loves heat and hates cold. Different varieties have wildly different aromas and flavour profiles — from classic sweet Italian to anise-scented Thai to the striking purple Opal. Pinch flowers constantly to keep producing leaves.
plantingGuide: Sow 5mm deep after soil warms above 18°C, or start indoors 4–6 weeks before transplanting. Barely cover seeds — warmth and light both aid germination. Pinch the growing tip when 6 true leaves appear to encourage bushy growth rather than a single tall stem.

name: Sweet Basil
description: The classic Italian kitchen basil. Large, glossy leaves with a sweet clove-like aroma. The essential ingredient in pesto and the perfect companion for tomatoes both in the garden and on the plate. Needs warmth to thrive — keep it near a wall or in a pot you can move indoors.
notes: Pinch out flower spikes immediately to keep leaves coming. Never refrigerate fresh basil — turns black. Store cut stems in water at room temperature.

name: Purple Basil
flavour: slightly spicier than sweet basil, with a clove note
description: Striking deep purple-black leaves that make an impact in the garden and on the plate. Slightly stronger and spicier than sweet basil. Excellent for making purple basil vinegar — steep leaves in white wine vinegar for a beautiful crimson condiment.
notes: Full purple colour develops best in bright sun. Flowers are also edible and look stunning as garnishes.

name: Thai Basil
bestUse: Thai curries, pho, stir-fries, spring rolls
description: A distinct variety with small, pointed leaves, purple stems, and a strong anise-licorice scent. More heat-tolerant than sweet basil and a staple in Thai, Vietnamese, and wider Southeast Asian cooking. Holds its flavour better than sweet basil when cooked.
notes: More heat and disease resistant than sweet basil. Don't substitute for Italian basil in pesto — the anise flavour is very different.

description: Mint is a vigorous perennial herb that spreads aggressively via underground runners. Always grow it in a container or with a physical barrier — it will take over a garden bed. Different varieties have very distinct aromas used in different culinary traditions.
plantingGuide: Plant container-grown specimens or divisions — never from seed, which produces variable plants. ALWAYS contain the roots: plant in a submerged bottomless pot, a pot sunk into the ground, or a raised bed with physical barriers. Division every 2–3 years keeps plants vigorous.

name: Peppermint
description: The most intensely aromatic mint, with high menthol content that creates that signature cooling sensation. A natural hybrid of water mint and spearmint. Used in everything from toothpaste to tea to cocktails. Grows vigorously in moist, part-shaded spots.
notes: MUST be contained — pot it or use a root barrier. Harvest before flowering for strongest flavour. Cut back hard in autumn; it will reshoot in spring.

name: Spearmint
bestUse: lamb sauce, tabbouleh, mint sauce, salads
flavour: sweet, mild mint — less menthol than peppermint
description: The culinary mint of choice for most savory dishes. Milder and sweeter than peppermint — less cooling, more complex. The classic choice for mint sauce with roast lamb, tabbouleh, and Middle Eastern cooking. Easier to use raw in salads without overpowering.
notes: The most common mint sold in supermarkets. If you only grow one mint, make it this one for kitchen versatility. Also contains in a pot.

description: Strawberries are among the most rewarding fruits for home gardeners. Compact plants spread via runners to form new plants. Different types fruit at different times — June-bearing (one big crop), everbearing (two crops), and day-neutral (continuous fruiting).
plantingGuide: Plant bare-root runners or potted plants with the crown exactly at soil level — never buried or too shallow. Space 30–40cm apart in rows 60cm apart. Mulch with straw after planting to keep fruit clean and retain moisture. Remove all flowers in the first season to redirect energy into root development.

name: Albion Strawberry
flavour: very sweet, dense flavour, firm texture
description: A premier day-neutral variety that fruits almost continuously from spring to frost. Large, firm berries with an intense sweet flavour. One of the best-tasting home garden strawberries. More disease-resistant than many heritage varieties.
notes: Remove runners in the first year to direct energy into fruit production. After year 2, let some runners establish new plants to renew the bed.

description: Blueberries are long-lived perennial shrubs that reward patient gardeners. They require acidic soil (pH 4.5–5.5) — a critical factor for success. Most varieties need a pollination partner. Once established, plants can produce for 20–30 years and require minimal care.
plantingGuide: Prepare soil thoroughly before planting — lower pH to 4.5–5.5 by incorporating sulfur and pine bark, or use a raised bed filled with ericaceous potting mix. Plant at the same depth as the container. Mulch deeply with pine bark or wood chips to maintain acidity and moisture. Always plant two varieties for cross-pollination.

name: Southern Highbush Blueberry
description: The best blueberry choice for Australian warm and temperate climates. Low chill hour requirements mean it fruits reliably without harsh winters. Large, sweet berries on compact bushes. Excellent varieties include 'Biloxi', 'O'Neal', and 'Sunshine Blue'.
notes: CRITICAL: Acidify soil to pH 4.5–5.5 with sulfur and pine bark mulch before planting. Use fertiliser formulated for acid-loving plants. Grows best in pairs for cross-pollination.

description: Raspberries grow on canes that produce fruit in their second year then die back. Each plant produces new canes (primocanes) each year. They spread via underground suckers to gradually fill a row. Most varieties prefer cool to temperate climates.
plantingGuide: Plant bare-root canes in winter or container plants in spring into fertile, well-drained soil. Space canes 40cm apart in rows 1.8m apart. Cut back to 20–25cm immediately after planting to focus energy on root establishment. Erect post-and-wire support (3 wires at 60, 90, 120cm) at planting time.

name: Heritage Raspberry
flavour: classic rich raspberry flavour, slightly tart
description: A popular everbearing (autumn-fruiting) variety that produces on new canes in its first year, then again the following summer. Large, firm berries with excellent flavour. One of the easiest raspberries to manage — simply cut all canes to the ground after autumn harvest.
notes: Tie canes to a wire or trellis. Everbearing varieties like Heritage are much simpler to prune than summer-only types — cut everything down in late winter.

plantingGuide: Direct sow only — carrots strongly dislike transplanting. Prepare deep, stone-free, loose soil and sow very thinly at 5mm depth. Thin to 5–8cm spacing once seedlings are 3cm tall; crowded roots fork and twist. Keep the bed moist until germination, which can take 2–3 weeks.

Plant seed potatoes (whole or cut to 2+ eyes) 10–15cm deep and 30cm apart. Chit them first — stand in a cool bright spot for 3–4 weeks until short sprouts develop. Earth up stems as they grow, mounding soil over the foliage 2–3 times, to prevent greening and increase yield.

Sow 2 seeds per position at 2cm depth in warm soil, or start indoors 2–3 weeks before transplanting. Thin to the strongest plant. Give each plant at least 1m² of space — they sprawl. If early fruits drop without setting, hand-pollinate: transfer pollen from a male flower (slender stem) to a female (with a tiny fruit at the base).

Direct sow 3–4cm deep after last frost when soil is above 16°C. Beans transplant poorly — always sow direct. Sow in blocks or wide rows rather than single lines to aid pollination. No fertiliser needed at planting; beans fix their own nitrogen.

Direct sow 3–4cm deep after last frost when soil is consistently warm. Erect a strong trellis, pole, or teepee structure at least 2m tall before sowing. Avoid cold, wet soil which causes seed rot. Sow 2–3 seeds per position and thin to the strongest one.

Direct sow 2–3cm deep in cool, moist soil from late winter onward — peas prefer cold conditions and tolerate hard frost. Sow in a double row either side of a trellis or netting and they'll find it themselves. Pre-soaking seed for a few hours speeds germination.

Direct sow 2–3cm deep in cool, moist soil. Set up trellis support at sowing time — snow peas need something to climb from an early stage. Sow every 4 weeks from late winter for a long harvest. Nitrogen fixers — no fertiliser needed at planting.

Direct sow 2–3cm deep in cool soil from late winter. Provide a trellis or netting at sowing time. Sugar snaps climb vigorously and need support from the start. Sow in succession every 3–4 weeks for continuous picking. No need for nitrogen fertiliser.

Direct sow 5cm deep in autumn or early spring. Plant in a grid pattern 20cm apart for self-supporting plants, or in rows with twiggy sticks for support. Pinch out the growing tip once the first pods set — this deters blackfly and directs energy into pod filling.

Raise in seed trays from late winter. Transplant when pencil-thick by dropping seedlings into deep holes (15cm) made with a dibber — do not backfill. Water the hole and let it fill naturally over time. This self-blanching method produces a long, white, tender shank.

Sow seeds 1cm deep in trays and transplant at pencil thickness, or plant sets (small bulbs) direct in early spring, pushing them in with just the tip showing. Keep beds weed-free — onions are poor competitors. Lift and dry thoroughly before storage.

Break bulbs into individual cloves and plant pointy-end up, 5cm deep and 15cm apart in autumn. This gives them a long cool period to develop before bolting in spring. Firm soil around each clove and mulch over winter. Always use certified disease-free planting stock.

Raise in cell trays and transplant at 5–6 weeks old. Prepare fertile, slightly alkaline soil (pH 6.5–7.0) — lime acidic soil well before planting. Firm transplants in well and water deeply. Use collars around stems to protect from cabbage root fly.

Raise in cell trays and transplant into firm, moist, fertile soil. Never let transplants dry out before planting or they may 'button' — form tiny heads immediately. Tie outer leaves loosely over the forming curd to blanch it white and protect it from frost and light.

Raise in cell trays and transplant into firm, fertile ground. Plant slightly deeper than the seedling was growing — up to the lowest leaf. Firm soil very well around the stem to prevent wind-rock. Use cardboard or purpose-made brassica collars to deter cabbage root fly.

Direct sow 1–2cm deep in cool, moist soil. Sow densely in a band 15cm wide rather than single rows. Thin to 15cm for large leaves, or leave dense for baby-leaf harvesting. Sow every 3 weeks from late summer through spring for a continuous supply.

Sow seed clusters 2cm deep, 30cm apart — each cluster contains 2–3 seeds, so thin to the strongest seedling once established. Very tolerant of different soil types and quite unfussy. Sow direct from late summer through spring.

Sow seed clusters 2cm deep, 10cm apart — each 'seed' is a cluster of 2–3 true seeds; thin to the strongest. Pre-soak seed overnight to speed germination. Sow successionally every 3 weeks for a continuous harvest. Tolerates light frost once established.

Direct sow 1cm deep, 3cm apart. Germination takes 3–5 days and harvest just 3–4 weeks — the fastest crop in the garden. Sow a short row every 2 weeks. Use as a row marker for slow-germinating crops like parsnip and carrot.

Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost. Like tomatoes, bury the stem slightly at transplanting. Eggplants love heat — use black plastic mulch to warm the soil. Stake at planting time. Never rush transplanting into cold ground; cold soil causes permanent stunting.

Direct sow 3–4cm deep after last frost in a block of at least 4×4 rows — corn is wind-pollinated and single rows produce poorly filled cobs. Thin to 30cm spacing. Water consistently as tassels emerge and silks develop, which is when pollination happens.

Sow 1cm deep in seed trays or direct into the bed. Transplant at 5–6 weeks to 45cm spacing. Firm in well — loose planting leads to poor anchorage and wind toppling. Very frost-hardy; flavour intensifies after a frost. Harvest outer leaves to encourage continued production.

Direct sow or transplant into moist, fertile soil in cool weather. Sow 1cm deep and thin to 20–25cm. Best in spring or autumn — summer heat causes rapid bolting. Sow in succession every 3 weeks. Can also be sown densely and harvested as baby leaves from 3–4 weeks.

Direct sow thickly in a band 5cm wide, 1cm deep — no thinning required. Sow every 4–6 weeks for a continuous supply. Harvest by pulling individual plants or cutting from 2cm above soil level to regrow. Also works as thinnings from onion seedbeds.

Plant individual bulbs (or sets) 3–4cm deep, pointy side up, 15–20cm apart. Plant in autumn for a late spring harvest, or early spring for summer. Each bulb divides into a cluster of 6–12 new shallots. Mulch after planting to retain moisture and suppress weeds.

Start indoors 10–12 weeks before transplanting — celery is slow from seed. Surface-sow (seeds need light) and keep evenly moist at 15–20°C. Transplant into very rich, consistently moist soil. Blanch stalks by wrapping with cardboard for 2–3 weeks before harvest for milder flavour.

Direct sow only — parsnips strongly dislike transplanting. Use fresh seed each season (old seed germinates poorly). Sow 1cm deep, 2–3 seeds per position, and thin to the strongest. Mark rows with fast-germinating radish, as parsnip can take 3–4 weeks to emerge.

Sow 5mm deep in moist seed compost. Germination is slow — allow 3–6 weeks. Pre-soak seed overnight in warm water to speed things up. Thin to 20–25cm. Parsley is biennial but usually grown as an annual — sow fresh each season for the best results.

Direct sow 1cm deep in cool weather — coriander bolts rapidly in heat. Sow every 3–4 weeks from early autumn through spring. Sow densely; a thick patch shades the soil and helps delay bolting. Crush the seed cluster between your fingers before sowing to separate the seeds.

Sow seed 5mm deep in spring or plant divisions from an established clump. Slow from seed — allow two seasons for a full productive clump. Plant 20cm apart in any reasonable soil. Cut back to 5cm height 2–3 times during the growing season to maintain fresh, tender growth.

Plant container-grown specimens in the warmest, sunniest, most well-drained spot available — ideally against a sunny wall. In heavy soil, plant in a raised mound amended with coarse grit. Space 60–90cm apart. Avoid clay or waterlogged ground which will kill it over winter.

Plant container-grown specimens or divide established clumps in spring. Needs excellent drainage — grows best at the edge of raised beds, on slopes, or in a rockery. In heavy soil, plant in a raised mound with added grit. Water sparingly once established.

Plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, sunny soil. Sage hates sitting wet in winter — raise the planting area or incorporate coarse grit. Space 45cm apart. Replace plants every 4–5 years as they become woody and less productive.

Direct sow 5mm deep where it is to grow — dill has a taproot and hates transplanting. Sow thinly in a sunny, sheltered spot (dill is wind-sensitive). Avoid sowing near fennel — they cross-pollinate and produce inferior hybrids. Let a few plants self-seed each season.

Plant container-grown specimens or raise from seed indoors at 20°C. Needs well-drained, lean soil — rich soil produces lax, flavourless growth. Space 30cm apart in full sun. Cut back hard after flowering each year to maintain compact, productive growth.

Direct sow 5mm deep in cool weather in a wide band for cut-and-come-again harvesting. Sow every 3–4 weeks from late summer through spring. Heat causes rapid bolting and very pungent leaves — sow in a shaded spot in summer or accept the spicier flavour.

Plant 1-year-old crowns in a prepared trench 20–30cm deep, spacing crowns 40cm apart. Drape the roots over a small mound of soil at the trench base. Cover with 10cm of soil and fill in further as shoots emerge. Do not harvest for the first 2 seasons — let all fronds grow to build the crown's reserves.
